volumeTitle: Amphitryon. The Comedy of Asses. The Pot of Gold. The Two Bacchises. The Captives
label: The Pot of Gold (Section prologue.pr.1 - Section 2.1.150c)

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.1
sections.original: Ne quis miretur qui sim , paucis eloquar .
sections.translation: Lest any one should wonder who I am , I will tell you in a few words . I am the household God of this family , from whose house you have seen me coming forth . It is now many years that I have been occupying this house , and I inhabited it

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.5
sections.original: patri avoque iam huius qui nunc hic habet .
sections.translation: for the father and the grandfather of this person who now dwells here . But beseeching me , his grandfather entrusted to me a treasure of gold , unknown to all . He deposited it in the midst of the hearth [*] , praying me that I would watch it for him . He , when he died , was of such an avaricious disposition ,

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.10
sections.original: numquam indicare id filio voluit suo ,
sections.translation: that he would never disclose it to his own son , and preferred rather to leave him in want than to show that treasure to that son . He left him no large quantity of land , on which to live with great laboriousness and in wretchedness .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.15
sections.original: ubi is óbiit mortem qui mihi id aurum credidit ,
sections.translation: When he died who had entrusted that gold to me , I began to take notice whether his son would any how pay greater honor to me than his father had paid me . But he was in the habit of venerating me still less and less by very much , and gave me a still less share of devotion .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.20
sections.original: item a me contra factum est , nam item obiit diem .
sections.translation: So in return was it done by me ; and he likewise ended his life . He left this person who now dwells here , his son , of the same disposition as his father and grandfather were . He has an only daughter ; she is always every day making offerings to me , either with incense , or wine , or something or other ;

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.25
sections.original: dat mihi coronas . eius honoris gratia
sections.translation: she presents me , too , with chaplets . Out of regard for her , I have caused this Euclio to find this treasure , in order that he might more readily give her in marriage if he should wish ; for a young man of very high rank has ravished her ; this young man knows who it is that he has ravished ;

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.30
sections.original: illa illum nescit , neque compressam autem pater .
sections.translation: she knows him not , nor yet does her father know that she has been ravished . This day I shall cause the old gentleman here , our neighbour , to ask her as his wife ; that will I do for this reason , that he may the more easily marry her who has ravished her . And this old gentleman who shall ask her as his wife ,

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.35
sections.original: is adulescentis illius est avonculus ,
sections.translation: the same is the uncle of that young man who debauched her in the night time at the festival of Ceres [*] . But this old fellow is now making an uproar in the house , as usual ; he is thrusting the old woman out of doors , that she may not be privy to the secret . I suppose he wants to look at the gold , if it be not stolen .

sections.label: Section 1.1.40
sections.original: Exi , ínquam . age exi . exeúndum herclé tibi hinc est foras ,
sections.translation: Get out , I say , be off , get out ; by my troth , you must budge out of this house here , you pryabout woman , with your inquisitive eyes .

sections.label: Section 1.1.42
sections.original: Nam cur me miseram verberas ?
sections.translation: Pray why are you beating me , wretched creature that I am ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.42b
sections.original: Vt misera sis
sections.translation: That you may be wretched , and that , curst as you are . you may pass a curst life , well befitting you .

sections.label: Section 1.1.44
sections.original: Nam qua me nunc causa extrusisti ex aedibus ?
sections.translation: But for what reason have you now pushed me out of the house ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.45
sections.original: Tibi egó rationem reddam , stimulorum seges ?
sections.translation: Am I to be giving you a reason , you whole harvest of whips [*] . Get away there from the door ! There , do look , if you please , how she does creep along . But do you know how matters stand with you ? If I just now take a stick or a whip in my hand , I ’ ll quicken that tortoise pace for you .

sections.label: Section 1.1.50
sections.original: Vtinam me divi adaxint ad suspendium
sections.translation: O that the Gods would drive me to hang myself , rather indeed than that I should be a slave in your house on these terms !

sections.label: Section 1.1.52
sections.original: At ut scelesta sola secum murmurat .
sections.translation: Hark how the hag is grumbling to herself ! By my troth , you wretch , I ’ ll knock out those eyes of yours , that you mayn ’ t be able to watch me , what business I ’ m about .

sections.label: Section 1.1.55
sections.original: abscede etiam nunc — etiam nunc — etiam — ohe ,
sections.translation: Get out ( pushes her with his hands ) — further yet ! still further ! further ! There now , stand you there ! By my faith , if you budge a finger ’ s breadth , or a nail ’ s width from that spot , or if you look back until I shall order you , i ’ faith , I ’ ll give you up at once as a trainer for the gibbet .

sections.label: Section 1.1.60
sections.original: scelestiorem me hac anu certo scio
sections.translation: I know for sure that I did never see one more accursed than this hag , and I ’ m sadly in fear of her , lest she should be cheating me unawares , or be scenting it out where the gold is concealed , a most vile wretch , who has eyes in the back of her head as well .

sections.label: Section 1.1.65
sections.original: nunc ibo ut visam sitne ita aurum ut condidi ,
sections.translation: Now I ’ ll go and see whether the gold is just as I concealed it , that so troubles wretched me in very many ways .

sections.label: Section 1.1.67
sections.original: Noenum mecastor quid ego ero dicam meo
sections.translation: By heavens , I cannot now conceive what misfortune , or what insanity , I am to say has befallen my master ; in such a way does he

sections.label: Section 1.1.70
sections.original: decies die uno saepe extrudit aedibus .
sections.translation: often , ten times in one day , in this fashion push wretched me out of the house . I ’ faith , I know not what craziness does possess this man ; whole nights is he on the watch ; then , too , all the day long does he sit for whole days together at home like a lame cobbler [*] .

sections.label: Section 1.1.74
sections.original: neque iam quo pacto celem erilis filiae
sections.translation: Nor can I imagine now by what means to conceal the disgrace of my master ’ s daughter , whose lying-in approaches near ; and there isn ’ t anything better for me , as I fancy , than to make one long capital letter [*] of myself , when I ’ ve tied up my neck in a halter .

sections.label: Section 1.2.79
sections.original: Nunc defaecato demum animo egredior domo ,
sections.translation: Now , with my mind at ease , at length I go out of my house ,

sections.label: Section 1.2.80
sections.original: postquam perspexi salva esse intus omnia .
sections.translation: after I ’ ve seen that everything is safe in-doors . Now do you return at once into the house ( to STAPHYLA ) , and keep watch in-doors .

sections.label: Section 1.2.81b
sections.original: Quippini ?
sections.translation: Keep watch in-doors upon nothing at all , forsooth ! or is it , that no one may carry the house away . For here in our house there ’ s nothing else for thieves to gain , so filled is it with emptiness [*] and cobwebs .

sections.label: Section 1.2.85
sections.original: Mirum quin tua me causa faciat Iuppiter
sections.translation: ’ Tis a wonder that , for your sake , Jupiter doesn ’ t now make me a King Philip , or a Darius [*] , you hag of hags . I choose those cobwebs to be watched for me . I am poor , I confess it — I put up with it . What the Gods send , I endure . Go in-doors , shut to the door , I shall be there directly .

sections.label: Section 1.2.90
sections.original: cave quémquam alienum in aedis intro miseris .
sections.translation: Take you care not to let any strange person into the house .

sections.label: Section 1.2.91
sections.original: quod quispiam ignem quaerat , extingui volo ,
sections.translation: What if any person asks for fire ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.92
sections.original: ne causae quid sit quod te quisquam quaeritet .
sections.translation: I wish it to be put out , [*] that there may be no cause for any one asking it of you . But if the fire shall be kept in , you yourself shall be forthwith extinguished . Then do you say that the water has run out [*] , if any one asks for it .

sections.label: Section 1.2.95
sections.original: cultrum , securim , pistillum , mortarium ,
sections.translation: The knife , the hatchet , the pestle and mortar , utensils that neighbours are always asking the loan of —

sections.label: Section 1.2.97
sections.original: fures venisse atque abstulisse dicito .
sections.translation: Say that thieves have come and carried them off . In fact , in my absence , I wish no one to be admitted into my house ; and this , too , do I tell you beforehand ,

sections.label: Section 1.2.100
sections.original: si Bona Fortuna veniat , ne intro miseris .
sections.translation: if Good Luck should come , don ’ t you admit her .

sections.label: Section 1.2.101
sections.original: Pol ea ipsa credo ne intro mittatur cavet ,
sections.translation: I ’ faith , she takes good care , I think , not to be admitted ; for though close at hand [*] , she has never come to our house .

sections.label: Section 1.2.103
sections.original: Tace atque abi intro .
sections.translation: Hold your tongue , and go in-doors .

sections.label: Section 1.2.103b
sections.original: Taceo atque abeo .—
sections.translation: I ’ ll hold my tongue , and be off .

sections.label: Section 1.2.103c
sections.original: Occlude sis
sections.translation: Shut the door , please , with both bolts . I shall be there directly .

sections.label: Section 1.2.105
sections.original: discrucior animi , quía ab domo abeundum est mihi .
sections.translation: I ’ m tormented in my mind , because I must go away from my house I ’ faith , I go but very unwillingly ; but I know full well what I ’ m about ; for the person that is our master of our ward [*] has given notice that he will distribute a didrachm of silver to each man ; if I relinquish that , and don ’ t ask for it , at once I fancy that all

sections.label: Section 1.2.110
sections.original: me suspicentur , credo , habere aurum domi .
sections.translation: will be suspecting that I ’ ve got gold at home ; for it isn ’ t very likely that a poor man would despise ever such a trifle , so as not to ask for his piece of money . For as it is , while I am carefully concealing it from all , lest they should know , all seem to know it ,

sections.label: Section 1.2.115
sections.original: omnes salutant quam salutabant prius ;
sections.translation: and all salute me more civilly than they formerly used to salute me ; they come up to me , they stop , they shake hands [*] ; they ask me how I am , what I ’ m doing , what business I ’ m about . Now I ’ ll go there whither I had set out [*] ; afterwards , I ’ ll betake myself back again home as fast as ever I can .

sections.label: Section 2.1.120
sections.original: Velím te arbitrári med haéc verba , fráter ,
sections.translation: I could wish you , brother , to think that I utter these words by reason of my own regard and your welfare , as is befitting your own sister to do . Although I ’ m not unaware that we women are accounted troublesome ; for we are all of us deservedly considered very talkative ,

sections.label: Section 2.1.125
sections.original: nec mutam profecto repertam ullam esse
sections.translation: and , in fact , they say at the present day that not a single woman has been found dumb [*] in any age . Still , brother , do you consider this one circumstance , that I am your nearest relation , and you in like manner are mine . How proper it is that I should counsel and advise you ,

sections.label: Section 2.1.130
sections.original: et mihi te et tibi me consulere et monere ;
sections.translation: and you me , as to what we may judge for the interest of each of us ; and for it not to be kept concealed or kept silence upon through apprehension , but rather that I should make you my confidant , and you me in like manner . For that reason , now , have I brought you here apart out of doors , that I might here discourse with you upon your private concerns .

sections.label: Section 2.1.135
sections.original: Da mi , optuma femina , manum .
sections.translation: Best of women , give me your hand .

sections.label: Section 2.1.136
sections.original: Vbi ea est ? quis ea est nam optuma ?
sections.translation: Where is she ? Who , pray , is this best of women ?

sections.label: Section 2.1.137
sections.original: Tu .
sections.translation: Yourself .

sections.label: Section 2.1.137b
sections.original: Túne ais ?
sections.translation: Do you say so ?

sections.label: Section 2.1.137c
sections.original: Si négas , nego .
sections.translation: If you say no , I say no .

sections.label: Section 2.1.138
sections.original: Decet te équidem vera próloqui ;
sections.translation: Indeed , it ’ s right that the truth should be spoken ; for the best of women can nowhere be found ;

sections.label: Section 2.1.140
sections.original: alia ália peior , fráter , est .
sections.translation: one is only worse than another , brother .

sections.label: Section 2.1.140b
sections.original: Idem ego árbitror ,
sections.translation: I think the same , and I ’ m determined never to contradict you on that point , sister . What do you wish ?

sections.label: Section 2.1.142
sections.original: Dá mihí óperam amabó .
sections.translation: Give me your attention , I beg of you .

sections.label: Section 2.1.142b
sections.original: Tuast , útere atque
sections.translation: ’ Tis at your service ; use and command me , please , if you wish for aught .

sections.label: Section 2.1.144
sections.original: Íd quod in rém tuam óptumum esse árbitror ,
sections.translation: A thing that I consider very greatly for your advantage

sections.label: Section 2.1.145
sections.original: téd id monitum advénto .
sections.translation: I ’ m come to recommend you .

sections.label: Section 2.1.146
sections.original: Soror , móre tuo facis .
sections.translation: Sister , you are doing after your usual manner .

sections.label: Section 2.1.146b
sections.original: Fáctum volo .
sections.translation: I wish it were done .

sections.label: Section 2.1.147
sections.original: Quid ést id , sorór ?
sections.translation: What is it , sister ?

sections.label: Section 2.1.147b
sections.original: Quod tibí sempitérnum
sections.translation: That you may enjoy [*] everlasting blessings in being the father of children .

sections.label: Section 2.1.149
sections.original: ( ita dí faxint ) volo te úxorem
sections.translation: May the Gods so grant it .

sections.label: Section 2.1.150b
sections.original: Ei occidí .
sections.translation: Ha ! I ’ m undone .

sections.label: Section 2.1.150c
sections.original: Quid ita ?
sections.translation: How so ?
